reviews.review_text:
I've spent about 2,000 hours playing the Legacy version of GTA V, and overall, I really enjoyed the game. The storyline is a masterpiece. Los Santos still holds a special place in my heart, and GTA Online can be a lot of fun, especially when playing with friends and just hanging out in the city. There’s simply a ton of content here - you’ll never run out of things to do.

Unfortunately, the online economy is a complete disaster. Over time, everything started to seem absurdly expensive - cars cost several million dollars, sometimes almost as much as entire businesses. Progress in online mode slowly turns into endless grinding, and the balance between rewards and prices seems completely broken.

The game bundle locks out new players from using their premium content due to account migration age restrictions. It is unplayable as advertised.  In order to claim the Starter Pack and "The Great White Shark" card benefit, the players need to play the legacy version of the game to the point of after character creation, and then, migrate to the Enhanced version of the game.  BUT there's a big catch:  In order for migration to work, the account needs to be at least 30 days old due to the "blanket way" of bot prevention.

Huge payout missions are designed to make it almost impossible to complete.  For example, the nightclub's sell mission that is approximately 1 millions payout would include delivery point that the huge delivery truck cannot pass through the bridge's gates if the truck is equipped with weapons on the roof, and if the weapons are removed from the roof and are installed with lesser versions, the mission would include the criteria that requires you to lose the cops upon delivery, which has a very high chance of failure due to insufficient weaponry.

This game manages to keep my attention despite having played it for multiple years now. The game is nearly a decade old and still has enough content to bring me back each time. If the story isn't my fancy, then I boot up the online mode and get obliterated by some Randoms. All in all, this is a 10/10 game, and I can't imagine any others being as chaotic yet perfect.
Unbelievable game, get side tracked all the time as there is a infinite amount of things to do/achieve. If GTA 6 if anything like this game it'll be the best game ever.
This game delivers chaotic fun, an exciting open world, and memorable characters. The missions, freedom, and online mode keep players entertained for hours despite the game’s age.

I remember playing Grand Theft Auto V on the Xbox 360 when it first came out as a young girl, and honestly the nostalgia alone carries this game hard for me. The story isn’t the best Rockstar has done, but it’s still entertaining enough to keep you hooked.

The real fun comes from just causing chaos, driving around the city, and messing with everything the game throws at you. It’s ridiculous, over-the-top, and somehow still fun all these years later.

7/10 — chaotic but fun.
superb game ! I play like 24 7. its super addictive. Nice map Nice story and very good game.!! I would give it 10 on 10!! Must Buy!!

GTA V was the most ambitious video game ever made at the time of its release. The single player story is excellent and, alongside environmental features, much of the beloved satire associated with the franchise makes for a darkly humorous narrative. 

Most of the hours I’ve spent in this game is accumulated from modded online servers accessed through mod clients like RAGE MP and FiveM. Roleplay servers are fun to play and I would suggest text rp over voice rp for realism and to avoid metagaming and poor RP, especially if you take RP seriously.

No matter what your intentions are playing this game, GTA V is one of the best sandbox free roam games.

After spending 2244 hours in Grand Theft Auto V, it honestly feels less like a game and more like a second home. I still remember the chaos of late-night heists with friends, random car meets at Los Santos Customs, and those moments where we’d spend hours doing absolutely nothing but somehow never get bored. Cruising through Los Santos while the radio played in the background hit differently back then, especially during the golden days of GTA Online. The game had this perfect mix of freedom, chaos, and unforgettable memories that very few games have ever matched. Even with all its flaws and grinding, GTA V created moments that felt genuinely special and kept me coming back year after year. No matter how many new games release, loading into Los Santos still brings back the same nostalgic feeling I had the first time I played it.

Pros and cons of GTAV Online.
Pros: Very fun, feels sandbox like, just with cops and players tryna slime you.
Very expansive map, tons of cool areas.
BEAUTIFUL weather and water systems.
Cons: Greifers
Almost ever heist/job requires a nightclub or smth like that.
Boring ways to get money, and 90% its not worth the work.
A classic, story mode is incredible, and online a lot of fun when not getting greifed. 10/10

This is the game that changed everything — three protagonists, massive heists, an insanely detailed Los Santos, sharp satire, and side content for days.
Niko from GTA IV had depth, but Franklin, Michael, and Trevor together created something special. Each brings their own style — Franklin's grounded ambition, Michael's family drama, Trevor's unpredictable madness. The story hits hard, the heists are unforgettable, and the world feels real.
It may show its age with rough textures and long load times, but this is the version that broke records and defined a generation. GTA V Legacy is a masterpiece and one of the best open-world games ever made.
10/10 for its time - The game that proved Rockstar was untouchable. Three stories, one legendary city.
No matter how good the game is the Rockstar Launcher and Rockstars sheer incompetence to make working online code just sours everything.

I must say, this is the most BEAUTIFUL and AMAZING game i have played (yet). I mean, i have a REALY bad pc, i have an intel i3 cpu and integrated graphics, but still, i get around 60 to 50 fps on medium graphics. The story is amazing, the graphics are stunning, and even okay fps. 10/10 would recommend even for the low spec gamers out there.

Even years after its initial release, GTA V remains the benchmark for open-world gaming. It is a sprawling, satirical, and adrenaline-fueled playground that offers endless hours of entertainment. Whether you are in it for the gripping narrative or the sheer chaos of Los Santos, this game delivers on every front.

Why I highly recommend it:

Three Protagonists, One Epic Story: The narrative structure of switching between Michael, Franklin, and Trevor is brilliant. It allows for a multi-layered story that keeps the pace fast and the perspectives diverse.

A Living, Breathing World: Los Santos is incredibly detailed. From the bustling city streets to the rugged wilderness, the world feels alive. You can lose yourself for hours just exploring or causing mayhem.

GTA Online: The online component is massive. With constant updates, heists, racing, and business management, it has evolved into a full-scale social and gaming platform that keeps millions of players coming back.

Sheer Variety: The gameplay variety is unmatched. You can pilot fighter jets, customize your cars, play golf, or pull off elaborate bank robberies. The freedom to do whatever you want is what makes this game timeless.

GTA V is a cultural phenomenon for a reason. It is polished, hilarious, and packed with content. If you haven't experienced the criminal underworld of Los Santos yet, you are missing out on one of the greatest games ever made.

Verdict: 10/10 – An absolute essential for every gamer's library.

Grand Theft Auto V is hands down one of the best open-world games ever made. Even after all these years, the graphics, story, gameplay, and attention to detail still feel amazing. Switching between Michael, Franklin, and Trevor keeps the story fresh and entertaining, and every character has their own unique personality that makes the game unforgettable.

The world of Los Santos feels alive — from random NPC interactions to crazy side missions and hidden activities, there’s always something to do. The driving mechanics, gunplay, and heist missions are incredibly fun, especially with friends in GTA Online.

What makes GTA 5 special is the freedom it gives players. You can follow the main story, explore the city, race cars, fly jets, start chaos, or simply relax and enjoy the environment. Rockstar truly created a masterpiece that still competes with modern games today.

Definitely worth playing if you love action, open-world exploration, and immersive storytelling.

GTA Online can be a very fun game when you get into the rhythm of it, which is its most frustrating aspect. The gameplay loop is bogged down by constant bugs and crashes, Shark Card-centric game design, lackluster anti-cheat. Those snags, especially in the hands of a studio that's likely ready to move on, snowball - and before you know it, you've started bargaining with the game, hoping a modder won't kick you mid-sell or that you won't get soft-locked during a heist prep. Overall, I don't regret the time investment, but it's hard to recommend a game that feels like it's working against you. Story mode is great, though.

Reality: I spent 4 hours running over pedestrians, running from a 5-star cop chase, and driving a supercar off Mount Chiliad while listening to 80s pop radio.

I know these games are lauded, but the ground movement and shooting controls (how you do most everything) was out of date when the game released. Now that it's been about 13 years... it is even worse. GTA IV felt more serious and in touch with controls given its release date than this ever did.

It's a fun sandbox, but god does the story suck and there is only so much driving around you can do before it just gets too boring. I couldn't bring myself to finish the campaign, too boring, too unserious,  too rockstar. 

Posting this review very late due to impending GTA 6 hype storm

GTA 5 is one of those games that feels impossible to review normally because it’s not just a game anymore, it’s basically a whole era of gaming. This game came out in 2013 and somehow still feels more alive than half the games that come out today with “next-gen” slapped on the box.

The story is still fire. Michael, Franklin, and Trevor all represent different types of chaos. Michael is the washed-up rich guy who “made it” but is still miserable. Franklin is trying to level up and escape the same street cycle. Trevor is just pure unfiltered demon energy. That three-character system still works because each of them gives you a different lens on Los Santos, and Los Santos itself is probably the real main character.

The map is insane. You can be in the hood, downtown, the hills, the desert, the mountains, the beach, or some random gas station in the middle of nowhere and it all feels like it has a purpose. Rockstar cooked with the atmosphere. Driving around at night listening to the radio still hits different. It has that weird feeling where even if you’re doing nothing, you’re still entertained.

The gameplay is simple but addictive. Shooting, driving, flying, robbing stores, causing chaos, running from cops, doing missions, buying cars, customizing weapons — it’s all easy to get lost in. And honestly, that’s the magic of GTA. You can play it like a serious crime movie or you can play it like a complete idiot and both are valid.

GTA Online is a different beast. It’s fun, but it also became this wild grind machine where everything costs millions and half the lobby is flying around on weaponized alien bikes like it’s no longer GTA, it’s capitalism with missiles. Still, with friends, it’s hilarious. Alone, it can feel like a second job.

My only real criticism is that Rockstar milked this game like crazy. GTA 5 is legendary, but bro, we were stuck with it for over a decade. At some point it became less “wow this game is timeless” and more “please release GTA 6 before society collapses.”

Overall, GTA 5 is still one of the greatest open-world games ever made. It has story, freedom, chaos, vibes, replay value, and that rare feeling where the world actually feels alive. It’s not perfect, and GTA Online got ridiculous, but the base game is certified legendary.

Rating: 9.5/10

The writing is elite, the satirical observations cut like a knife, and Trevor.... Well he is indeed a sick puppy. His Stockholm Syndrome romance arc was one of the best moments in all of gaming. "No gasolina!"

9/10

GTA 5 wasn’t just a game to me, it was years of memories. Countless late nights with friends, nonstop laughs, stupid moments that somehow became unforgettable, and friendships that were built through chaos in Los Santos. From random car meets to insane heists that went completely wrong, every session felt like a new story.

No matter how old the game got, it always brought people together. GTA 5 gave me some of the best gaming memories I’ll ever have, and I’ll always look back on it as one of the greatest games ever made.

Got this game in December 2024. Only really started to play at the beginning of this year. Now I'm a multi-millionaire with an impressive wardrobe and collection of cars, lol Once I got into it I haven't been able to stop playing it. Tip- Play in Invite Only sessions to get all your missions and sales done. That's the only way my hubby and I have ever played. We don't mess with public lobbies. I LOVE this game under those conditions. Even solo, this method works and provides hours of fun and entertainment. 

I love the open world aspect of online. I've never played anything but the opening Lamar and Franklin race in Story Mode. The open world online suits my playstyle better. Even as an older game the graphics are good, and the gameplay feels satisfying. It can feel a bit grindy at times, but there is always something to do. You feel a great sense of accomplishment and progression as your skills improve and you begin to acquire businesses, cars, the mansion, etc. I'm sure I'll eventually burn myself out, but for now I'm living the high life and loving it. 

I'm looking forward to GTA Online 6, but as I understand it, that is probably a year or two away for us PC players. Oh well, I'll enjoy the events, perks, bonuses, and gameplay on V as long as I can. I'm hoping, praying, and fingers crossed that the new game won't get woked up and dumbed down. The racy, satirical humor is outstanding. Definitely not for snowflakes. I PRAY they won't take all that creativity and sass away from the new game. The language and jokes in V are clever, real, and make the immersion even more compelling. Definitely more relatable to IRL if one were actually in those types of environments and situations.

So glad hubby talked me into really starting to seriously play. We play Legacy as I don't want to risk losing my progress or account trying to migrate Legacy to Enhanced. Even still it's run mostly fine and we continue to have tons of fun with it.

GTA 5 is technically better than San Andreas in almost every way. But there's one thing that keeps pulling me back to that older game, and GTA 5 never came close to filling that gap.
Objectively GTA 5 is impressive. Los Santos feels dense and alive, the three protagonist structure with overlapping storylines was a genuinely fresh idea, and as open worlds go this is still one of the best ever built. All of that is real.
But every time I finish a mission, drive home, and sit there with nothing to do that actually connects me to the world, I think about how expressive San Andreas was in something that sounds small but matters a lot for immersion which is relationships.
In San Andreas CJ could date, could actually court someone, take them to a restaurant or a drive-in, get a call from a girlfriend when he'd been away too long, even get dumped if he kept ignoring her. None of that was gameplay-critical but all of it made San Andreas feel personal. It made me feel like I lived there instead of just passing through on missions.
GTA 5 barely has any of that. Michael has a family but the interactions are shallow and mostly cutscene-locked. Trevor is too chaotic to feel grounded. Franklin basically exists in an emotional vacuum for the whole game. No dating activities, no relationships that grow through actual play, no character tying me to the world in a way that feels earned outside of scripted story beats.
The world in GTA 5 is bigger and more beautiful but I always felt more like a tourist in it than someone who belonged there.
Honest criticism and suggestion: Rockstar already proved they can build something with real emotional texture through Red Dead Redemption 2 and its relationship systems. I hope GTA 6 brings that same energy to Los Santos, not just a bigger map or sharper visuals but actual space for players to feel genuinely connected to the characters and the world around them in ways that go beyond the main story.

13 years and still fun to play, other than the money system the game is amazing.
